nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich reefs in the Western Indian Ocean, from the Gulf of Aden south to Natal, South Africa, including Seychelles, Comoros, and Madagascar. Typically encountered at depths of roughly 1–25 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on algae, small invertebrates, and sponge-based matter. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of Spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and other meaty items. Access to mature live rock for grazing is generally advised.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 2549x1699. Tack-sharp full lateral profile of a live individual in native Madagascar reef; clearly shows the diagnostic bright yellow (goldtail) caudal fin, blue-masked face with orange eye-ring/ear-spot, and numerous thin pale curved bars. Cleanest single-subject framing.

Diego Delso, 5514x3676. Sharp, highly vibrant left-facing view showing the orange lower belly, electric-blue face lines, yellow tail and yellow/white curved bars. Different individual and angle for variety.
Diego Delso, 4359x2906. Sharp full-body profile of another live in-situ individual; yellow tail, blue face mask, ear-spot and curved barring all clearly visible.

summary: The Chrysurus Angelfish is a large, boldly patterned species from the Western Indian Ocean, recognized by its yellow tail, tan body with blue-edged markings, and dark ear-spot. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty; semi-aggressive and best kept singly in a large, mature system.
careNotes: The Chrysurus Angelfish, also sold as the Goldtail Angelfish and occasionally the Ear Spot Angelfish, is a large member of the family Pomacanthidae (Pomacanthus chrysurus) native to the Western Indian Ocean, ranging from the Gulf of Aden south to Natal, South Africa. It requires a spacious, mature aquarium — a minimum of around 1,000 litres with plenty of live rock for grazing and open water for swimming. Water should be kept at a salinity of 1.020–1.025 specific gravity, pH 8.1–8.4, and carbonate hardness of 8–12 dKH. Like other large Pomacanthus angelfishes, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite in the wild. It has been known to nip at stony and soft corals and at the mantles of tridacna clams, so it is generally not considered fully reef-safe; some keepers report success alongside small-polyped stony corals and noxious soft corals, but caution is advised in any mixed reef setting. Large angelfish of this group tend to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and the sensory line running down the body — frequent feedings with marine algae, vitamin-enriched foods, and consistently stable water chemistry are generally advised to reduce the risk. A notable behavioral trait: when stressed, this species has been documented making an unusual clicking sound. It is best kept as the sole angelfish in the system, as conspecific aggression toward other angelfish tends to be high.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered extremely difficult and has not been reliably documented for home aquariums. In the wild this species spawns pelagically as a protogynous hermaphrodite. No home-aquarium breeding protocol is described in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max size (33.0 cm SL, consistent with LiveAquaria's 13 inches), native range (Western Indian Ocean, Gulf of Aden to Natal, South Africa, including Seychelles, Comoros, Madagascar), depth (1–25 m), and temperature (26–28.9°C). Temperature is higher than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); averaged to 24–27°C. Notes species is seldom seen in the aquarium trade, reef-associated, non-migratory, and rated Least Concern by IUCN.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner in the wild; captive breeding has not been reliably documented for home aquariums and is considered extremely difficult.
